title: Stollen
subtitle: German Christmas enriched bread with marzipan and dried fruit
excerpt: A dense, spiced Christmas loaf with soaked dried fruit, blanched almonds, and a marzipan log sealed inside the dough. Brushed with butter straight from the oven and buried under icing sugar. Keeps and improves for two weeks wrapped in foil.
sourceNotes: Stollen is a German Christmas enriched bread documented in European household manuals from the fifteenth century. Domestic German baking texts from the nineteenth century, available through the Internet Archive, describe an enriched yeasted dough with dried fruit, candied peel, ground spice, and a marzipan core, finished with butter and icing sugar after baking. The recipe appears in English in Mrs Rundell, A New System of Domestic Cookery (1806), Project Gutenberg, which describes the German spiced enriched Christmas loaf. Hannah Glasse, The Art of Cookery Made Plain and Easy (1747), Project Gutenberg: enriched fruit loaves with mixed spice, shaped before a second prove and finished with sugar, are documented in the chapter on celebration breads.

recipe.freezeNotes: Wrap in foil and freeze. Defrost at room temperature overnight. The icing sugar can be refreshed with a light dusting after defrosting.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Stollen improves significantly over the first week as the fruit oils migrate into the crumb. Make at least 3 days ahead if possible; wrap tightly in foil and store at room temperature.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, middle shelf. Low temperature is essential — the dense, fruit-laden dough needs a long, gentle bake to cook through without burning the surface

glossaryTerms.definition: The shaping technique for stollen: the dough is rolled into a flat oval, a marzipan log is placed just off centre, and one half of the dough is folded over it and pressed down firmly to seal. The fold creates the characteristic asymmetric hump and ensures the marzipan stays enclosed during baking.

content.text: Stollen is the German Christmas bread: dense, heavily spiced, and fruit-laden, with a log of marzipan sealed inside the dough. It is not a light bake. The crumb is compact and rich, the crust disappears under a thick layer of icing sugar and butter, and the whole loaf improves over a week as the fruit and spice oils work into the dough. Make it at least three days before you plan to eat it.
Soak the fruit, at least 2 hours

Combine the {{raisins}}, {{sultanas}}, {{mixed-peel}}, and {{blanched-almonds}} in a bowl. Pour over the {{kirsch}} if using, stir, cover, and leave for at least 2 hours (overnight is better). If not using kirsch, the fruit goes in dry.

Combine the {{plain-flour}}, {{yeast-fast-action}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, {{caster-sugar}}, {{mixed-spice}}, and {{cardamom-green}} in a large bowl. Beat the {{eggs}} with the {{whole-milk}}, {{unsalted-butter}} for the dough, and {{vanilla-extract}}. Pour into the flour and mix to a smooth, quite stiff dough. Knead for 8 minutes until the dough comes together. Add the soaked fruit and nuts and knead until evenly distributed — the dough will seem lumpy but will smooth as you work.
Cover and leave in a warm place for 60 minutes. The enriched fruit-laden dough rises more slowly than a lean dough; expect a modest increase rather than a full double.

Mix the {{almond-flour}}, {{icing-sugar}} for the marzipan, and {{egg-whites}} together until a firm paste forms. Shape into a log about 20 cm long and 3 cm in diameter. If it is too sticky, chill for 10 minutes.

Line a baking tray with baking paper. On a lightly floured surface, roll the dough into an oval roughly 30 × 20 cm. Place the marzipan log just off-centre along the length. Fold one half of the dough over the marzipan and press the edge down firmly to seal. This is the
. Transfer to the lined tray. The loaf should have a rounded hump on one side where the marzipan sits.
Cover loosely and leave for 30 minutes. Preheat the oven to 170°C fan.

Bake for 40–45 minutes until the crust is deep brown and a skewer inserted into the centre (avoiding the marzipan) comes out clean. The internal temperature should read 92°C. As soon as the stollen comes out of the oven, brush it all over with the melted {{unsalted-butter}} for finishing — the hot crumb absorbs it immediately. Leave for 5 minutes, then sift the {{icing-sugar}} for finishing over the top in a thick, even layer. It should look white. Cool completely on a rack, then wrap tightly in foil.
